entry.title: RAG From Scratch: Express, React, and the Free Gemini API

link.title: RAG From Scratch: Express, React, and the Free Gemini API

entry.content:
Large language models are confident by default — including when they’re wrong. Ask one about something outside its training data, your private notes, or last week’s news, and it will often answer anyway, fluently and incorrectly. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is the standard fix: instead of trusting the model’s memory, you hand it the relevant facts at answer time and ask it to work from those.

This post covers the theory in brief, then walks through running and understanding a minimal but complete RAG implementation — rag-demo, an Express + React app wired to the free Gemini API. It’s the same project I used in a recent session at Eldohub Eldoret on 3rd July 2026 — photos from the event here — and this write-up is the companion reference for anyone who wants to rebuild it step by step.

Why RAG?

Before the code, it’s worth being clear about what problem RAG actually solves. Three gaps show up whenever you rely on a model’s memory alone:

  • Knowledge cutoff — training data has a fixed end date. Anything that happened after that date simply doesn’t exist for the model.
  • No access to private data — a model can’t answer questions about your own notes, docs, or codebase, because it was never trained on them.
  • Hallucination — when a model is uncertain, it tends to generate a plausible-sounding answer rather than admit it doesn’t know.

RAG addresses all three the same way: retrieve the relevant text from a knowledge source you control, insert it into the prompt as context, and instruct the model to answer from that context — and to say so if the context doesn’t contain the answer. The model still does the writing; it just stops writing from memory alone.

The mechanism has three steps:

  1. Retrieve — find the chunks of stored text most relevant to the question.
  2. Augment — insert those chunks into the prompt as grounding evidence.
  3. Generate — the model answers using the supplied context.

Steps 1 and 2 are what separate RAG from a plain chatbot, and they’re what this demo makes visible.

How Retrieval Actually Works

Retrieval depends on two ideas: embeddings and vector similarity.

An embedding is a numeric representation of text — a long list of floating-point numbers — produced by an embedding model such that texts with similar meaning end up close together in that numeric space, regardless of the exact words used. “Affordable airfare” and “cheap flights” land near each other even though they share no words.

To find relevant chunks for a question, the app embeds the question and compares it against every stored chunk’s embedding using cosine similarity — the cosine of the angle between two vectors. A smaller angle means a higher similarity score. The top-scoring chunks are the ones handed to the model as context.

A vector database is just infrastructure built around this idea: store embeddings alongside their source text, and answer “which vectors are closest to this one?” quickly, even across millions of records, using approximate nearest-neighbor indexes instead of comparing against every vector one by one. This demo skips the index — it’s a plain array with a cosine similarity function — because at a few hundred chunks, brute force is fast enough to see the mechanism clearly. The concept is identical to what Pinecone, Chroma, pgvector, or Weaviate do at scale.

Setting Up the Demo

The full source is at github.com/franksitawa/rag-demo. It’s a two-process app: an Express backend that handles embedding, storage, and retrieval, and a React (Vite) frontend for adding documents and asking questions.

rag-demo/
  backend/
    server.js        # Express routes: /api/documents, /api/query
    gemini.js         # Calls Gemini embedContent + generateContent REST endpoints
    vectorStore.js     # In-memory chunking + cosine similarity search
    .env.example
    package.json
  frontend/
    src/App.jsx        # UI: knowledge base panel + query panel + pipeline trace
    src/App.css
    src/index.css
    package.json
    vite.config.js     # Proxies /api to the backend during dev

1. Get a free Gemini API key

Go to aistudio.google.com/apikey and create a key. Google AI Studio’s free tier is generous enough to run this demo without paying anything.

2. Clone the repo and start the backend

git clone https://github.com/franksitawa/rag-demo.git
cd rag-demo/backend
npm install
cp .env.example .env
# edit .env and paste your GEMINI_API_KEY
npm start

The server runs on http://localhost:3001.

3. Start the frontend

In a second terminal:

cd rag-demo/frontend
npm install
npm run dev

Open the URL Vite prints — usually http://localhost:5173.

4. Try it

  1. Paste a chunk of text into the Knowledge base panel — an article, some notes, anything — and click Add to knowledge base. Add a few separate pieces if you like; each is chunked and embedded independently.
  2. Type a question related to what you pasted and click Ask.
  3. Read the answer, then check the retrieved sources underneath — you’ll see exactly which chunks were pulled in and their similarity scores.

Ask something the pasted text doesn’t cover, too. The prompt instructs Gemini to say it doesn’t have enough information rather than guess — that instruction only works because retrieval is doing its job correctly.

Walking Through the Code

gemini.js — talking to the API

Both embedding and generation go through Gemini’s REST API directly, with no SDK dependency. The key detail is taskType: chunks being stored use RETRIEVAL_DOCUMENT, while the user’s question uses RETRIEVAL_QUERY. Gemini optimizes the resulting vectors differently depending on which side of the search they’re going to be on.

export async function embedText(text, taskType = "RETRIEVAL_DOCUMENT") {
  const apiKey = getApiKey();
  const url = `${GEMINI_BASE}/models/${EMBEDDING_MODEL}:embedContent?key=${apiKey}`;

  const res = await fetch(url, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
    body: JSON.stringify({
      model: `models/${EMBEDDING_MODEL}`,
      content: { parts: [{ text }] },
      taskType,
    }),
  });

  const data = await res.json();
  return data.embedding.values; // array of floats
}

Generation is a separate call — generateAnswer(prompt) — that sends a fully-assembled prompt (question plus retrieved context) to generateContent and returns the model’s text response.

vectorStore.js — the retrieval logic

This file is the entire “vector database” in the demo: chunking, storage, and cosine similarity search, in about sixty lines.

Chunking splits long text into overlapping pieces so an idea that straddles a boundary isn’t cut in half:

export function chunkText(text, chunkSize = 500, overlap = 80) {
  const cleaned = text.replace(/\s+/g, " ").trim();
  const chunks = [];
  let start = 0;
  while (start < cleaned.length) {
    const end = Math.min(start + chunkSize, cleaned.length);
    chunks.push(cleaned.slice(start, end).trim());
    if (end === cleaned.length) break;
    start = end - overlap;
  }
  return chunks.filter(Boolean);
}

Similarity search compares the query vector against every stored vector and returns the highest-scoring matches:

function cosineSimilarity(a, b) {
  let dot = 0, normA = 0, normB = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < a.length; i++) {
    dot += a[i] * b[i];
    normA += a[i] * a[i];
    normB += b[i] * b[i];
  }
  return dot / (Math.sqrt(normA) * Math.sqrt(normB));
}

export function similaritySearch(queryVector, k = 4) {
  const scored = store.map((item) => ({
    ...item,
    score: cosineSimilarity(queryVector, item.vector),
  }));
  scored.sort((a, b) => b.score - a.score);
  return scored.slice(0, k);
}

Swap this file for a call to Pinecone or pgvector and nothing else in the app needs to change — that’s the point of separating retrieval logic from the rest of the pipeline.

server.js — wiring it together

Two routes do all the work. POST /api/documents chunks and embeds pasted text:

app.post("/api/documents", async (req, res) => {
  const { text, source } = req.body;
  const chunks = chunkText(text);
  const vectors = await embedBatch(chunks, "RETRIEVAL_DOCUMENT");
  const added = chunks.map((chunkTextValue, i) =>
    addChunk(chunkTextValue, vectors[i], source || "pasted-text")
  );
  res.json({ chunks: added, totalChunksInStore: storeSize() });
});

POST /api/query is the retrieval-augmentation-generation sequence in one place: embed the question, retrieve the top chunks, build a grounded prompt, and generate the answer.

app.post("/api/query", async (req, res) => {
  const { question, k = 4 } = req.body;

  const queryVector = await embedText(question, "RETRIEVAL_QUERY");
  const topChunks = similaritySearch(queryVector, k);

  const context = topChunks
    .map((c, i) => `[Source ${i + 1}] ${c.text}`)
    .join("\n\n");

  const prompt = `Answer using ONLY the context below. If the answer isn't
there, say you don't have enough information.

Context:
${context}

Question: ${question}
Answer:`;

  const answer = await generateAnswer(prompt);
  res.json({ answer, sources: topChunks });
});

Everything upstream of this route — chunking, embedding, storage — exists to make this one function call possible: by the time the prompt is built, the model only has to reason over a handful of relevant paragraphs instead of guessing from its training data.

App.jsx — showing the mechanism, not hiding it

The frontend deliberately doesn’t hide the pipeline behind a plain chat box. A small trace animates through ingest → embed → store and embed query → retrieve → augment → generate as each request runs, and every answer is shown next to the exact chunks and similarity scores that produced it. For a demo whose whole purpose is explaining how RAG works, that visibility matters more than a polished chat UI would.

How This Maps to Production RAG

The demo keeps every piece simple enough to read in one sitting, which means it also cuts corners a production system wouldn’t:

This demo Production equivalent
In-memory array + cosine similarity Pinecone, Chroma, pgvector, Weaviate, Qdrant, etc.
Fixed-size character chunking with overlap Semantic/structural chunking (by heading, sentence, token count)
Chunks re-embedded on every restart, nothing persisted Persisted vector DB with incremental indexing
Single free-tier key, sequential embedding calls Batched calls, rate limiting, retries

None of those upgrades change the underlying pattern — retrieve, augment, generate — they just make it hold up at scale.

Where to Go From Here

If you want to extend the demo, a few directions are natural next steps:

  • Swap the vector store for pgvector or Chroma so the knowledge base survives a restart.
  • Add metadata filtering — tag chunks by source or date and filter before running similarity search.
  • Chain it into a pipeline — feed it documents from n8n or a scheduled job instead of pasting text by hand, the same way the XTTS voiceover service plugs into an automated video pipeline.

The full code is on GitHub at franksitawa/rag-demo — clone it, add your own notes to the knowledge base, and see what it gets right and where it falls back to “I don’t have enough information.”
entry.summary: Why Retrieval-Augmented Generation matters, how to run the rag-demo starter repo locally, and a walkthrough of the code that makes retrieval, embedding, and grounded generation work together.

entry.title: Building a Custom XTTS Service for Manim Voiceover

link.title: Building a Custom XTTS Service for Manim Voiceover

entry.content:
Manim Voiceover ships with built-in support for services like OpenAI and Azure, but if you are running XTTS locally in Docker, none of those apply. What you need is a custom service that talks directly to your local XTTS API.

This post walks through building one. If you have not yet set up XTTS or Manim Voiceover, start with these two posts first:

  • Running XTTS-v2 with Docker and NVIDIA CUDA on Ubuntu
  • Installing Manim and Manim Voiceover

How the Plugin Works

Manim Voiceover expects a speech service that extends SpeechService and implements a single method: generate_from_text. When your scene hits a with self.voiceover(...) block, the plugin calls this method, receives a path to the generated audio file, measures the duration, and exposes it as tracker.duration so your animation can sync to it.

That contract is all you need to implement.

The Custom Service

Create a file named xtts_service.py in your project directory:

import requests
from manim_voiceover.services.base import SpeechService


class XTTSService(SpeechService):

    def __init__(
        self,
        api_url="http://localhost:8020/tts_to_audio",
        speaker_wav="speaker.wav",
        language="en"
    ):
        super().__init__()
        self.api_url = api_url
        self.speaker_wav = speaker_wav
        self.language = language

    def generate_from_text(
        self,
        text,
        cache_dir=None,
        path=None,
        **kwargs
    ):
        payload = {
            "text": text,
            "speaker_wav": self.speaker_wav,
            "language": self.language
        }

        response = requests.post(self.api_url, json=payload)
        response.raise_for_status()

        with open(path, "wb") as f:
            f.write(response.content)

        return {
            "original_text": text,
            "input_text": text,
            "output_file": path
        }

The implementation is intentionally minimal. It sends the text and speaker configuration to XTTS, writes the returned audio bytes to path, and returns the metadata dictionary Manim Voiceover expects.

Note: The exact API endpoint may differ depending on which XTTS server image you are running. The daswer123/xtts-api-server image used in the Docker setup post exposes /tts_to_audio by default. Check your container’s docs at http://localhost:8020/docs if you get a 404.

Using the Service in a Scene

With xtts_service.py in the same directory as your scene file, you can import and use it directly:

from manim import *
from manim_voiceover import VoiceoverScene
from xtts_service import XTTSService


class BinarySearchVideo(VoiceoverScene):

    def construct(self):
        self.set_speech_service(XTTSService())

        title = Text("Binary Search")

        with self.voiceover(
            text="Binary search repeatedly cuts the search space in half."
        ) as tracker:
            self.play(
                Write(title),
                run_time=tracker.duration
            )

tracker.duration is derived from the actual length of the audio clip XTTS generates. This means the animation timing adjusts automatically to match the narration — no hardcoded run_time values, no manual syncing.

Why Not Just Use a Cloud Service?

The built-in OpenAI and Azure services work, but they introduce dependencies that matter at scale:

  • Cost — every render call bills against your API quota. When you are iterating on a scene and re-rendering dozens of times, this adds up fast.
  • Latency — cloud round-trips slow down your development loop.
  • Voice consistency — cloud TTS voices are fixed. With XTTS you clone a specific voice once and reuse it across every video in your series, giving your content a consistent identity.
  • Offline capability — once the Docker container is running, the pipeline works without an internet connection.

For a one-person content production setup, eliminating API costs and keeping everything local makes the workflow significantly more sustainable.

Extending the Service

A few useful additions once you have the basics working:

Switch voices per scene — pass a different speaker_wav when instantiating XTTSService() to use different voices for different types of content.

Add caching — XTTS generation takes a moment. If you render the same voiceover line repeatedly during development, caching the audio file by a hash of the text string avoids redundant API calls.

Plug into a pipeline — because the service is plain Python, it can be called from n8n, LangGraph, or any automation layer. You can build a workflow where lesson scripts are generated by an LLM, passed to Manim scenes, and rendered to video with narration — all without manual intervention.

The Bigger Picture

This custom service is the bridge between the XTTS Docker setup and Manim’s animation pipeline. Once it is in place, the full workflow from text to narrated video runs entirely from Python, entirely on your local machine:

  1. Write the lesson script
  2. Define the Manim scene
  3. Render — XTTS generates audio, Manim syncs and composites
  4. FFmpeg handles final output formatting

That is the foundation I use for producing animated CS explainers, and it scales cleanly as the content library grows.
entry.summary: How to build a custom Manim Voiceover speech service that connects to a locally running XTTS Docker container — giving you free, offline narration with voice cloning.

entry.title: Bubble Sort Visualizer
entry.content:
Bubble Sort Visualizer

Watch Bubble Sort compare adjacent elements and swap them into place.

Ready
entry.summary: An interactive Bubble Sort visualizer — step through comparisons, play at your own pace, or skip a full pass. Colour-coded bars show every swap in real time.

entry.title: Installing Manim and Manim Voiceover for AI-Narrated Educational Videos

link.title: Installing Manim and Manim Voiceover for AI-Narrated Educational Videos

entry.content:
Manim is the animation engine originally built by 3Blue1Brown for mathematical explainers. The community edition makes it accessible to anyone building educational content with Python. Combined with the Manim Voiceover plugin, you can synchronise generated speech directly with your animations — no manual audio editing required.

This guide covers installation on Ubuntu and a custom XTTS integration that generates narration locally, without cloud API costs.

Prerequisites

Install the system dependencies Manim needs for video rendering, font support, and LaTeX:

sudo apt update

sudo apt install -y \
    ffmpeg \
    libcairo2-dev \
    pkg-config \
    python3-dev \
    pango1.0-tools \
    texlive \
    texlive-latex-extra

Set Up a Virtual Environment

Isolate your project dependencies in a virtual environment:

python3 -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install --upgrade pip

Install Manim

pip install manim
manim --version

The terminal should print the installed version, confirming Manim is ready.

Install Manim Voiceover

The Voiceover plugin handles speech generation and synchronises audio timing with animations automatically:

pip install manim-voiceover
pip show manim-voiceover

Install Audio Dependencies

The plugin requires SoX for audio processing. Without it you will see warnings like:

WARNING  SoX could not be found!

Install it with:

sudo apt install -y sox

Test Your Installation

Create a file named hello.py:

from manim import *

class HelloWorld(Scene):
    def construct(self):
        text = Text("Hello Manim")
        self.play(Write(text))
        self.wait()

Render and preview it:

manim -pql hello.py HelloWorld

Manim will render the animation and open it automatically. If you see the text appear on screen, your installation is working correctly.

Speech Provider Options

Manim Voiceover supports several speech backends. Install only the ones you need.

For OpenAI:

pip install openai

For Azure:

pip install azure-cognitiveservices-speech

In my own workflow, I use a custom XTTS service that connects to a locally running XTTS Docker container. If you missed that setup, you can find it here: Running XTTS-v2 with Docker and NVIDIA CUDA on Ubuntu.

This approach gives you natural-sounding narration at zero API cost, with no audio data leaving your machine.

A Narrated Animation Scene

Once installed, adding voiceover to a scene is straightforward. Here is a minimal example using the XTTS service:

from manim import *
from manim_voiceover import VoiceoverScene
from xtts_service import XTTSService

class SlidingWindowVideo(VoiceoverScene):

    def construct(self):
        self.set_speech_service(XTTSService())

        title = Text("Sliding Window")

        with self.voiceover(
            text="Today we learn the sliding window technique."
        ) as tracker:
            self.play(
                Write(title),
                run_time=tracker.duration
            )

        self.wait()

The tracker.duration value comes from the generated audio clip, so the animation automatically stretches or compresses to match the narration length — no manual tweaking required.

What Comes Next

With Manim and Voiceover in place, the full production workflow becomes:

  1. Write the lesson as a Python scene
  2. The XTTS service generates narration from your script
  3. Manim renders animation and audio into a single video file
  4. FFmpeg handles any final cuts or formatting

This is the same pipeline I use to produce educational CS content — animated explainers on topics like Big O notation, sorting algorithms, and data structures — entirely from a local machine, with no cloud dependencies.
entry.summary: A step-by-step guide to installing Manim Community Edition and the Manim Voiceover plugin on Ubuntu, including a custom XTTS integration for cost-free local narration.

entry.title: Running XTTS-v2 with Docker and NVIDIA CUDA on Ubuntu

link.title: Running XTTS-v2 with Docker and NVIDIA CUDA on Ubuntu

entry.content:
XTTS-v2 from Coqui is one of the most capable open-source TTS systems available today. It supports multilingual synthesis and voice cloning from a short audio sample — all running locally on your hardware.

This guide walks through running XTTS-v2 inside Docker on Ubuntu with CUDA-accelerated GPU inference, which makes speech generation dramatically faster than CPU-only execution.

Prerequisites

Before starting, make sure you have:

  • Ubuntu 22.04 or 24.04
  • An NVIDIA GPU with drivers installed
  • Docker and Docker Compose installed

Verify your GPU is visible to the system:

nvidia-smi

You should see a table showing your GPU name, driver version, and memory usage. If this command fails, install your NVIDIA drivers first before proceeding.

Step 1: Install Docker

sudo apt update
sudo apt install docker.io -y
sudo systemctl enable docker
sudo systemctl start docker
docker --version

Step 2: Install Docker Compose

sudo apt install docker-compose-plugin -y
docker compose version

Step 3: Install the NVIDIA Container Toolkit

By default, Docker containers cannot access your GPU. You can confirm this by running:

docker info | grep -i runtime

If the output only shows runc and not nvidia, Docker has no GPU support yet.

Add the NVIDIA repository and install the toolkit:

curl -fsSL https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/gpgkey | \
  sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg

curl -s -L \
  https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/stable/deb/nvidia-container-toolkit.list | \
  sed 's#deb https://#deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg] https://#g' | \
  sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/nvidia-container-toolkit.list

sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y nvidia-container-toolkit
sudo nvidia-ctk runtime configure --runtime=docker
sudo systemctl restart docker

Verify the NVIDIA runtime is now registered:

docker info | grep -i runtime
# Expected: Runtimes: io.containerd.runc.v2 nvidia runc

Step 4: Test GPU Access from Docker

Before deploying XTTS, confirm that Docker can see the GPU:

docker run --rm --gpus all \
  nvidia/cuda:12.2.0-base-ubuntu22.04 \
  nvidia-smi

If you see GPU info printed from inside the container, you are ready to proceed.

Step 5: Create the Project Structure

mkdir xtts && cd xtts
mkdir speakers output

Your directory should look like this:

xtts/
├── speakers/
└── output/

The speakers/ folder will hold your voice reference WAV files, and output/ is where generated audio will be written.

Step 6: Create the Docker Compose File

Create docker-compose.yml inside the xtts/ directory:

services:
  xtts:
    image: daswer123/xtts-api-server
    container_name: xtts

    ports:
      - "8020:8020"

    volumes:
      - ./speakers:/app/speakers
      - ./output:/app/output
      - xtts_models:/app/models

    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: all
              capabilities: [gpu]

    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  xtts_models:

Note: Do not override the container’s command: field. The daswer123/xtts-api-server image uses a custom entrypoint, and overriding it with python -m xtts_api_server causes an exec: python: not found error. Let the image use its defaults.

Step 7: Start the Container

sudo docker compose up -d
sudo docker ps

Check that the container is running, then follow the logs:

sudo docker logs -f xtts

The first startup will download the XTTS-v2 model, which may take a few minutes.

Step 8: Add a Voice Sample

Record a clean WAV file of the voice you want to clone — 6 to 30 seconds of clear speech works best. Tools like Audacity or OBS Studio work well on Ubuntu.

Place the file in:

speakers/frank.wav

Verify the container can see it:

docker exec -it xtts ls /app/speakers
# frank.wav

Step 9: Generate Speech

Open the API docs in your browser:

http://localhost:8020/docs

Send a request to the /tts_to_file or /tts_to_audio endpoint with a payload like:

{
  "text": "Welcome to my YouTube channel.",
  "speaker_wav": "frank.wav",
  "language": "en"
}

XTTS will clone the voice from frank.wav and return synthesised audio.

Troubleshooting

could not select device driver "nvidia" with capabilities: [[gpu]]

The NVIDIA Container Toolkit is not installed or not configured. Re-run Step 3.

exec: python: not found

You added a command: override in docker-compose.yml. Remove it and let the image use its default entrypoint.

Failed to open the input "/app/example/frank.wav"

The WAV file is not in the right place or the volume is not mapped correctly. Confirm:

  • The file is at speakers/frank.wav on the host
  • The volume in docker-compose.yml is ./speakers:/app/speakers

no configuration file provided: not found

You are running docker compose up from the wrong directory. Navigate to the folder containing docker-compose.yml first:

cd xtts
docker compose up -d

What You Can Build With This

Once XTTS-v2 is running, it becomes a powerful building block for a local AI media stack:

  • AI assistants with a cloned or custom voice
  • Audiobook generation from text or EPUB files
  • YouTube narration pipelines — combine with faster-whisper for transcription and FFmpeg for final rendering
  • Automated content creation workflows driven by n8n or LangGraph agents

XTTS-v2 runs entirely on your machine, with no API costs and no data leaving your environment.

Frank Sitawa is an AI & Fullstack Engineer based in Nairobi, Kenya.
entry.summary: A practical guide to running Coqui XTTS-v2 locally using Docker and NVIDIA GPU acceleration on Ubuntu — with GPU setup, Docker Compose config, and common troubleshooting fixes.

entry.title: AI Agents: What Happens When AI Starts Taking Action

link.title: AI Agents: What Happens When AI Starts Taking Action

entry.content:
A chatbot answers questions. An AI agent does something more interesting — it makes decisions, uses tools, and takes a sequence of steps to complete a goal.

If LLMs are the brain, agents are what happen when you give that brain hands.

What Makes Something an Agent?

A basic language model takes input and produces output. One turn, done.

An agent operates in a loop:

  1. It receives a goal.
  2. It decides what action to take.
  3. It takes that action — searching the web, writing code, calling an API, reading a file.
  4. It looks at the result.
  5. It decides what to do next.

This continues until the goal is complete — or the agent gets stuck.

A Concrete Example

Say you ask an AI agent: “Research the top three AI tools for recruiters and summarise them in a table.”

A chatbot would try to answer from memory, probably giving you outdated information.

An agent would:

  • Search the web for recent articles on AI recruitment tools
  • Open and read several pages
  • Compare the tools across features, pricing, and reviews
  • Write a summary table based on what it actually found

Same question, very different process.

The Tools Agents Use

Agents get access to tools — functions they can call to interact with the world. Common ones include:

  • Web search — find current information
  • Code execution — write and run code to solve problems
  • File reading/writing — work with documents
  • API calls — interact with external services like calendars, databases, or email

The agent decides which tool to use and when, based on the goal and what it’s learned so far.

Why This Is a Big Deal

Most real-world tasks aren’t single questions — they’re processes. Research, then analyse, then draft, then send. Agents can handle multi-step workflows that would otherwise require a human in the loop at every stage.

This is why agents are showing up in software engineering, customer support, and business operations — anywhere a task has multiple steps that currently need a human at every stage.

What to Watch Out For

Agents need guardrails. An agent that can take actions can also take the wrong ones — deleting files, sending premature emails, or looping on a task it can’t complete.

Good agent design includes human checkpoints, especially for high-stakes tasks. Autonomy and oversight work together, not against each other.

Frank Sitawa is an AI & Fullstack Engineer based in Nairobi, Kenya.
entry.summary: A chatbot answers questions. An AI agent goes further — it makes decisions, uses tools, and takes steps to complete a goal. Here's how that works.

entry.title: What Is a Large Language Model and How Does It Actually Work?

link.title: What Is a Large Language Model and How Does It Actually Work?

entry.content:
ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude — you’ve heard the names. They’re all Large Language Models, or LLMs. But what does that actually mean? And how does a piece of software produce text that sounds like a human wrote it?

Let’s break it down without the jargon.

It Starts With Prediction

At its core, an LLM does one thing: it predicts the next word.

Given the sentence “The capital of France is…“, it predicts “Paris”. Given “She opened the door and…“, it predicts something that fits — “walked in”, “screamed”, whatever makes sense in context.

That sounds simple. But do this billions of times, across hundreds of billions of words of text, and something remarkable happens: the model starts to develop a deep sense of language, facts, reasoning patterns, even tone.

How It Learns

LLMs are trained on massive amounts of text — books, websites, code, articles. During training, the model reads a sentence, tries to predict the next word, checks if it got it right, and adjusts its internal settings slightly based on the error. Repeat this trillions of times.

The model never memorises text word for word. Instead, it learns patterns — grammar, facts, how arguments are structured, how questions are typically answered.

What “Large” Actually Means

The “large” in Large Language Model refers to the number of parameters — the internal numerical settings the model adjusts during training. GPT-4 is estimated to have over a trillion parameters. More parameters generally means more capacity to capture nuance and handle complex tasks.

More parameters also means more computing power needed, which is why training frontier models costs tens of millions of dollars.

Why They Sound So Human

Because they’re trained on human-written text. The model has seen how humans explain things, argue, joke, apologise, and teach. It learned to mirror those patterns.

It doesn’t understand in the way you do. There’s no meaning behind the words for the model — just statistical relationships between tokens. But those relationships are rich enough to produce output that’s often indistinguishable from human writing.

The Limitation to Keep in Mind

LLMs are frozen at training time. They don’t learn from your conversations, and they don’t know what happened after their training cutoff. That’s exactly why RAG (see the previous post) exists — to give them access to fresh, specific information at query time.

Frank Sitawa is an AI & Fullstack Engineer based in Nairobi, Kenya.
entry.summary: ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude — they're all Large Language Models. But what does that actually mean, and how do they produce such convincing text?

entry.title: How AI Finds and Uses Information: A Beginner’s Guide to RAG

link.title: How AI Finds and Uses Information: A Beginner’s Guide to RAG

entry.content:
Ever asked an AI chatbot a question and gotten a confidently wrong answer? That’s not a random glitch — it’s a fundamental limitation of how most AI models work. And the fix has a name: RAG.

RAG stands for Retrieval-Augmented Generation. The idea is simple once you see it.

The Problem: AI Works From Memory Alone

A standard AI language model is like a very well-read person who studied everything on the internet up to a certain date — then went into a room with no phone, no books, and no Wi-Fi.

They’ll answer from memory. But if something changed after they went in, they won’t know. And if they half-remember something, they’ll fill in the gaps with a guess — and sound completely confident doing it. Researchers call this hallucination.

The Fix: Give the AI a Way to Look Things Up

RAG adds a retrieval step before the AI responds:

  1. You ask a question.
  2. The system searches a knowledge base — documents, PDFs, records — and pulls the most relevant pieces.
  3. Those pieces get passed to the AI alongside your question.
  4. The AI answers using both what it knows and what it just retrieved.

The AI is no longer working from memory alone.

A Simple Analogy

You’re a customer support agent. A customer asks about the current savings interest rate. You don’t guess — you search the company handbook and give an accurate answer.

That’s RAG. The handbook is the knowledge base. The search is retrieval. Your response is generation.

Why It Matters

  • Stays current — update the knowledge base without retraining the model.
  • More accurate — the AI works from real documents, not reconstructed memory.
  • Transparent — you can show users exactly which source the answer came from.

This is why RAG powers legal research tools, medical assistants, enterprise knowledge bases, and customer support bots.

What RAG Doesn’t Fix

RAG is powerful but not magic. If the knowledge base has bad information, the AI will use it. If retrieval pulls the wrong documents, the answer suffers. Think of it as giving the AI a library card — whether it finds the right book still matters.

Frank Sitawa is an AI & Fullstack Engineer based in Nairobi, Kenya.
entry.summary: Ever wondered why ChatGPT sometimes makes things up — and how smarter AI systems get around that? The answer is RAG.
